fr:mainmatter.html:p: In addition to the assessment tasks (discussed below), the unit consists of lectures and tutorials.

You're in the lectures right now; they are written in form, and interlinked. The Table of Contents is a great place to get an overview of the semester. There are 12 numbered lectures and 12 teaching weeks of the semester: a lucky coincidence! Each week, you should make sure you read that week's lecture and understand it as well as you can before you come to tutorial. (That said, if you haven't read the lecture on a given week, you are still very much welcome to come to tutorial!)

The lectures are written (rather than audio or video) so that they can serve a double purpose: they will introduce you to our material, but they will also serve as reference material for you. You do not need to memorise anything in this unit; you can always go back to the lectures and check.

(At least if you're in one of my tutes, you may well get sick of hearing me say "check the lectures" in response to some questions. But I only say it so much because it's good advice! When you have a question about exactly how something works, the answer is often in the lectures. Going through the work of digging the answer up when you need it will help you learn, much more than me just telling you the answer would.)

These lectures are an evolving thing; this semester marks a big shift: from pdf to the web-based format you're currently looking at, which uses the excellent Forester tool. Some lectures are not yet converted over; they will appear as we go.

If you find any errors or face any difficulties with these lectures, please let me know! These lectures have benefitted tremendously from the experiences, suggestions, and ideas that past students have offered. Future students can benefit from your experiences as well; just let me know any sugggestions!

Tutorials are where most of your learning will happen in this unit. No attendance is taken at tutorials, and no part of your semester mark depends on them; they are pure learning opportunities for you and nothing more. Each week, your tutor will give you a range of exercises (more straightforward) and puzzles (more devious) that you'll work on in various ways, and also answer any questions you have about the material.
These are the lectures for Monash ATS2866, running in Semester 1 2026, taught by Ellie Ripley. Welcome!
If you just want to check out the lectures, feel free to poke around. This note is about the structure of the unit, its assessment, etc.
In addition to the assessment tasks (discussed below), the unit consists of lectures and tutorials.

You're in the lectures right now; they are written in form, and interlinked. The Table of Contents is a great place to get an overview of the semester. There are 12 numbered lectures and 12 teaching weeks of the semester: a lucky coincidence! Each week, you should make sure you read that week's lecture and understand it as well as you can before you come to tutorial. (That said, if you haven't read the lecture on a given week, you are still very much welcome to come to tutorial!)

The lectures are written (rather than audio or video) so that they can serve a double purpose: they will introduce you to our material, but they will also serve as reference material for you. You do not need to memorise anything in this unit; you can always go back to the lectures and check.

(At least if you're in one of my tutes, you may well get sick of hearing me say "check the lectures" in response to some questions. But I only say it so much because it's good advice! When you have a question about exactly how something works, the answer is often in the lectures. Going through the work of digging the answer up when you need it will help you learn, much more than me just telling you the answer would.)

These lectures are an evolving thing; this semester marks a big shift: from pdf to the web-based format you're currently looking at, which uses the excellent Forester tool. Some lectures are not yet converted over; they will appear as we go.

If you find any errors or face any difficulties with these lectures, please let me know! These lectures have benefitted tremendously from the experiences, suggestions, and ideas that past students have offered. Future students can benefit from your experiences as well; just let me know any sugggestions!

Tutorials are where most of your learning will happen in this unit. No attendance is taken at tutorials, and no part of your semester mark depends on them; they are pure learning opportunities for you and nothing more. Each week, your tutor will give you a range of exercises (more straightforward) and puzzles (more devious) that you'll work on in various ways, and also answer any questions you have about the material.
Your mark this semester is determined by four components. Here, I say a bit about each, in increasing order of difficulty.
There is a Moodle quiz on the content of each lecture. These quizzes are multiple choice and allow for unlimited attempts; only your best mark counts. Due dates are as listed on the Moodle.
These quizzes are basic and overviewy; they look just at main ideas. The purpose of these quizzes is twofold:

html:ul.html:li: To make sure that you get credit for keeping up with the unit and grasping the main ideas
To help reinforce the main points of the lectures in your mind

fr:mainmatter.html:p: NOTE: Binchicken is currently down for between-semester maintenance. It will be up in the first few weeks of semester, and you will receive an announcement. Please do not email me about this.

You will do a number of your logic exercises at the website https://binchicken.one. This is a site that I've made to automatically generate and mark a range of different types of logic exercises. This semester, there will be ten different exercise types in total. Each exercise type has three ranks you can reach; to earn the full 35%, reach rank 3 in every exercise type. You cannot lose ranks once you gain them.

To reach ranks 1 and 2 in any exercise type, all that matters is the total number of exercises of that type that you've gotten correct. Incorrect responses don't count for you or against you, so you can try as many exercises as you like of any given type until you reach the needed total correct. To reach rank 3 of any exercise type, you need to get a streak of correct answers of a certain length. For rank 3, any incorrect answers will break your streak, and you'll need to start again; but incorrect answers don't count against you other than that. There is no limit to the number of exercises you can do; each is randomly generated, so you will never run out.

It's important to me to avoid, as much as I can, penalising you for getting things wrong. Learning logic, like learning any other skill, takes practice. Practice often looks like getting things wrong for a while before starting to get them right. I wouldn't want to push you away from practicing!

That's why the Binchicken marks work as they do: once you can get a streak of a certain length correct, you've shown that you understand that exercise type well enough for full marks on it. I don't care at all how many mistaken attempts it takes you to get there! I just want to help you get there.

There are two workshops timetabled for this semester. These will be whole-class in-person activities where you'll have group-based tasks to complete. One will be an escape room and one a murder mystery. Details will be provided in class and at the workshops themselves. Each workshop is worth 10% of the semester, for a total of 20%.

By the nature of these workshops, of course, it's not possible to have an extension in the usual way---they happen at the time they happen, and when they're over they're over. If you do need special consideration for one of these workshops, we'll arrange an oral exam as a substitute assessment.
The most challenging (and correspondingly most interesting) part of your assessment will be a puzzle-solving session, where you and another student will work together on solving a logical puzzle.

This is a brand-new form of assessment this semester, so you are the guinea pigs for it. I'll include example puzzles of the kind that will feature in this session in the lectures, and give you ideas of approaches that you can use to work on them.

A rubric for this session, and the procedure we'll use to schedule them, will be made available in the first few weeks of class. Like the workshops, it doesn't really make sense to have extensions for these; any approved special consideration will have to take the form of an alternate assessment, again an oral exam.

html:ul.html:li:
Section A: Preliminaries
Lecture 1: Introduction and languages
Lecture 2: Functions and substitutions
Lecture 3: Sets, arguments, and logics

Section B: Structures and matrices
Lecture 4: Structures
[Lecture 5: Matrices and counterexamples]
[Lecture 6: Using matrices to compare logics]
[Lecture 7: The limits of matrices]

Section C: Exploring intuitionistic logic
Lecture 8: Natural deduction
Lecture 9: Intuitionistic logic
Lecture 10: Intuitionistic models
Lecture 11: Harmony
